steps.prompt: Which word is a relative pronoun?

hints.reveletur: 'Reveletur' is a verb meaning 'may be revealed'

steps.reveal: quod = which (nom. neut. sg., relative pronoun)

hints.accusative: While 'quod' is identical in accusative neuter, here it is nominative subject of 'reveletur'
hints.ablative: Ablative neuter would be 'quo' — 'quod' is nominative
hints.conjunction (because): 'Quod' can be a conjunction, but here it introduces a relative clause

steps.reveal: opertum = covered / hidden (perf. pass. part., nom. neut. sg.)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'operiens' — 'opertum' is perfect passive
hints.future active participle: Future active would be 'operturus' — 'opertum' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'operiendus' — 'opertum' is perfect passive participle

hints.aperio, aperire, aperui, apertum: These are the parts of 'aperire' (to open) — the opposite of 'operire' (to cover)
hints.opero, operare, operavi, operatum: These would be from 'operari' (to work) — different verb from 'operire'
hints.reperio, reperire, repperi, repertum: These are the parts of 'reperire' (to find/discover)

hints.to rebel: That would be 'rebellare' — 'reveletur' comes from 'revelare' (to reveal)

steps.reveal: reveletur = may be revealed / will be revealed

hints.present indicative passive: Present indicative passive would be 'revelatur' — 'reveletur' with -e- is subjunctive (1st conj.)
hints.future indicative passive: Future passive would be 'revelabitur' — 'reveletur' is present subjunctive
hints.imperfect subjunctive passive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'revelaretur' — 'reveletur' is present subjunctive

steps.prompt: Why is the subjunctive used in 'reveletur'?
steps.target: relative clause of characteristic (describing a type)
choices: relative clause of characteristic (describing a type)

hints.purpose clause: A purpose clause uses 'ut' + subjunctive — here 'quod' introduces a relative clause of characteristic
hints.result clause: A result clause uses 'ut' + subjunctive — here the subjunctive is in a characteristic relative clause
hints.indirect question: An indirect question would use an interrogative word — 'quod' is relative, not interrogative

hints.velo, velare, velavi, velatum: These are the parts of 'velare' (to veil) — 'revelare' is its compound re + velare
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.removeo, removere, removi, remotum: These are the parts of 'removere' (to remove)

hints.accusative: While 'nihil' is identical in accusative, here it is the subject — nominative
hints.genitive: 'Nihil' is indeclinable (or sometimes 'nihili' in gen.) — here it is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'nihilo' — 'nihil' is nominative subject

translation: Nothing is hidden that will not be revealed
